text: The main physiological variables determining long-distance running performance are VO2max, velocity at VO2max (vVO2max), running economy, and lactate threshold variables.

In prediction models for long-distance running performance, 43.4% of independent variables pertain to aerobic metabolism, 26.5% to training load, and 20.6% to anthropometric variables, body composition, and somatotype.
specific_value: 43.4% aerobic metabolism, 26.5% training load, 20.6% anthropometric/body composition

text: The strongest predictor variables for half-marathon and marathon performance are laboratory-derived measures (VO2max, vVO2max), training variables (training pace, training load), and anthropometric variables (fat mass, skinfolds).
Anthropometric variables including fat mass and skinfold thickness are significant predictors of half-marathon and marathon performance.

Half-marathon performance prediction models rely mainly on anthropometric variables but with only moderate coefficients of determination, suggesting limited predictive power from body composition alone.
Marathon performance prediction relies fundamentally on training variables and physiological evaluation combined with anthropometric parameters, rather than any single factor alone.

Physiological effort assessments (laboratory-based) are used almost exclusively for predicting performance in shorter distance specialties (5000m and 10,000m), while field-test-based prediction models for longer distances remain underdeveloped.
